expectations.text: A criterion benchmark file is produced testing both linear scan and indexed approaches
expectations.evidence: bench_search.rs contains 5 benchmark groups comparing linear_scan vs hashmap_index vs hashmap_sorted_index across multiple query patterns.

expectations.text: The indexed approach uses HashMap<Uuid, Vec<&Pago>> or similar pre-built index
expectations.evidence: alternatives.rs defines PagoIndex with `by_contrato: HashMap<Uuid, Vec<&'a Pago>>` and PagoIndexSorted with the same plus sorted buckets.

expectations.text: The benchmark tests the most common query pattern (filter by contrato_id)
expectations.evidence: bench_by_contrato_id is the first benchmark group, testing the 80% case with `Some(target_contrato)` as the filter.

expectations.text: The benchmark accounts for index construction cost (not just query time in isolation)
expectations.evidence: bench_index_build group explicitly benchmarks `PagoIndex::new(black_box(&pagos))` and `PagoIndexSorted::new(black_box(&pagos))` separately.

expectations.text: Test data is generated at production scale (~5000 pagos)
expectations.evidence: generate_pagos(5000) is used for all main benchmarks. Scaling benchmark also tests 100, 500, 1000, 5000.

expectations.text: The output discusses the amortization tradeoff: index build cost vs number of queries before rebuild
expectations.evidence: report.md discusses: 'Index build cost is ~50-100μs... If the index is built once and reused across many queries (e.g., cached in application state and rebuilt on data change), the amortization is excellent: even 10 queries recoup the build cost.'
